speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Byers Anderson took place at his home in Bovill on November 13, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I had to get back up where the snow was. I hear my feet crunch in the snow.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now it's a problem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get overseas during World War I?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I was on the Gulf of Mexico.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Our main camp was Pensacola and what was the name of this other place?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, it was out from Gulfport there, out of the Expedition buildings. Our headquarters is there, there's a big airfield there. Then there was Pensacola was the principal airport.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you enlist or did you get drafted?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I was enlisted. I was a mechanic on the airplanes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Some difference between them airplanes now and then. You could hear them old crates whistle as they'd come down and regular kites.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do as a mechanic on them? Is there much you could do to make them run?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, you worked on the Liberty Motor and the Hispano. Hispano was a French motor and Liberty Motor and then we had another motor. I forget the name. It was some difference in what it is today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were made out of spruce, weren't they? Weren't they made out of spruce, part of those old planes?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, they were made out of wood and had wire struts coming down to the side. They were made out of wood and they was, you'd take the potlatch, reserved all the, well the government reserved all the spruce that was in the country here. Had every spruce tree in the country marked.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They didn't last long though. Of course the spruce, if they'd have a crash landing or anything, they'd splinter up steady, cracking up. They'd bend and they'd break all right but they wouldn't pull apart.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well I'd heard that that had something to do with the timber production going on in the woods right then. It did. They were really pushing to get the spruce out of these woods. Oh yes, yes. They was getting the spruce out and picking out choice stuff. That's the airplanes, but they was principally made of old spruce. That's what I say, there's a regular kite.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Listen, I want to ask you some more about Bill Geary. You mentioned to me that he was a pretty good salesman. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's his story on that? He actually did some selling himself?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yes he sent his, well I don't know whether it was his nephew, I think it was his nephew, that he sent him back east to sell white pine and white fur. He went back there and he didn't get a half a dozen orders. Well the mill was going to either have to shut down or they was going to get something. No Bill, he fired him, put him on another job. Bobby didn't fire him, he put him on another job and says I'll go back myself. He went back and called it Silver Pine. He put it out I think around 18, 20 dollars a thousand. He just sold carload after carload and kept the mill running through them hard times and come out with it and made money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those eastern dudes didn't know what Silver Pine was or they thought it was something fancy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, they didn't know what Silver Pine was. They thought it was a valuable wood. Well it was and it is. You can take and build a house with white fur and it's the best building material you can get right today for the inside working stuff of a house. It will hold up and after the boards, good boards, it won't crack like the white pine and other stuff. Of course now they've got preservatives that they put on their lumber and it preserves it. Then my white fur was cheap and they could build the inside and the outside of the house and then covered and they had good building materials they could get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that, did you say that he didn't get along too well with Featherstone? Was he the first superintendent?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, he was the first superintendent the potlatch ever had in here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He'd get his men to working, an old building, come up. At that time he traveled mostly by what they called a hack,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: buggy team.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he'd come up and change his men all around. He'd have his men working doing such things and Featherstone was kind of an independent old cotcher anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, Bill, he says, why do you always come up and change my men around for?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says, I've got them doing as I want them to do. And he says, you come up here and you wreck my whole plans. Bill says, I want them that way. Made old Frank mad and he says, well, he says, maybe you'd better take the whole works and run it. Well, Bill says, I can do that too. He was a hard -boiled old customer.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Featherstone quit and old Bill went to Spokane and he met T .P. Jones coming down this street. And he told T .P., he says, you're just the man I'm looking for. He says, I want you to come and be the superintendent of the potlatch. Did he know who T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Did he know T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, he knew him back in Cloquet, Minnesota.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Jones was a railroad engineer and he worked in the camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was Jones doing out here in Spokane?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he was hunting for work when they shut down and stuff up there and he just got to come west, you know. And Jones didn't want it at first and finally old Deary talked him into it. Well, he come down and took over and he was a wonderful old fella to work for.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He'd argue with it but if you convinced him you was right, he'd give in to you. Most of them fellas won't give in to you, you know, whether they're right or wrong. They wanted it as they wanted. But T .P. wasn't that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now, like the men working at the camps and up here, Camp 8, that is the first driving outfit that they had in here before the railroad got here. Well, he told the men they could have a saw, a crosscut saw, a shovel, a spittin' maul, and a couple wedges, and an axe. But he says, you take any more and he says, I'll figure it, you're stealin'. And he says, I'll handle it as such. Well, everybody had a saw and a snow shovel and a spade and axe.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And then they even hauled wood in, loaded on the train and rolled it off at their places. They had houses along the track at Slabtown there. They'd stop the train, roll off two, three logs along for each one, and they'd saw them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that okay by T .P.?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they'd cut those old white pine, you know, dead. Old bed tamerac. They never used any of that wood at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did these families move in to Slabtown and Collin?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They moved in there. That'd go back into 1956.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They settled up in here. At 1904, I think they started work on the dam for the railroad. They never thought of getting in here, and they made the dam up here. In 1904, I believe it was over until they told me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was working for potl - until he was working for potlatch then?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, it was all potlatch then. Well, these first men in there at Collin's in Slabtown, now, did you say that the fries were the - They were in there really right early. Oh, yes. They was in there in the 90s. And that was the outpost for the mail being delivered. Old Corwin was the - that lives down here out from Pullman there, was the mail - He was 14 years old driving mail truck. Yeah, I spoke to him. Yeah. Well, Fry, now, was he - did you say that he was independent at first, and he started - he started - he had his own sawmill and not - he wasn't part of potlatch when he was - Oh, no. No, he was never part of the potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had a little store in the post office and a couple of three buildings there at - Oh, what was the name of that place he called it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he run the store, and there was a schoolhouse there. And then old Abe Fry, he had a homestead, and Jake Fry had a homestead, John Fry, and then they all had homesteads right around. And Collins there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they'd come up and turn around there with the mail and go on back down to Troy. They'd come out from Troy. The railroad would come to Troy, usually, and they'd get their mail.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And old Abe Fry, he sold out. He sold his - he's the one that made the money at that time. He sold his homestead for ten thousand dollars. He come out smelling like a rose at that time. I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He sold it to somebody else or two?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He sold it to the potlatch. They must have wanted it pretty bad for him. He had timber on there that White Pine was four feet through, just like hair on a dog. And then Sam sold his - later sold his homestead. He didn't sell them the homestead, but he sold them the timber off of it. And he got four dollars a thousand for cutting it and hauling it and loading it down to the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't make out so good as his brother did, eh?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, but he made money at that. You could really do something with money at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of people were the Fry's? Were they a real independent bunch of men?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. It sounds like they weren't too anxious to go to work for the company. No, no. They didn't work for the company at all. They was an independent outfit. Of course, old Sam raised cattle.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he, not in a big amount, but he had a few cattle and he had his horses. He'd done farming and he had his store. And they made a good living. And one of the girls - not the girls, but his wife taught school. So they had no worries at that time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And then when the railroad comes through,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: they picked up quite a lot of work from the railroad, hauling freight and stuff that way. They had a regular freight outfit hauling in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there actually much of a settlement at Collins of Holmes?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yes. They was - I don't remember how many people there, but they was the Fry's. Jake Fry and his outfit. And then there was Abe Fry and old John Fry.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And then they had the school and the store and it was an outpost. Sounds like a family town. Yeah, it was. Good deal. But there was lots of outsiders that come in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About when Potlatch came in or before?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yeah, before the Potlatch came in. And after the railroad got in and everything,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: they'd stop and put their freight off right at Collins.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But the Fry's, they was pretty ambitious. There was nothing lazy about them at all. They was workers, but they was independent.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And then when they left here, they went to Moscow and got that old white hotel. They were on the corner at the intersection down there by the service station. And they run that. Well, I guess they still own it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any of the old timers left of the Fry's?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, yes, I think the girls are all alive. I run into them every little while there in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Slabtown? When did that come in?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, I think Slabtown was built in - Oh, they was 19 -4 or 5. You see, Sam put in a little sawmill there. And that's where it got its name, Slabtown. And they built a store and a post office. Then they had about a dozen houses there. And old Dave Gentry, he was always a cattleman, but Dave worked for the potlatch when the potlatch come in.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he bought and sold cattle on the side. He had a cabin across the river from Slabtown.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he had a lot of people there. They used to have quite a time there at Slabtown.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They'd walk and last through the steers and ride them bareback. They always had a rope tied onto them so that the steers couldn't gore them though. As a kid, I'd go out there and we'd ride them and they'd get bucked off. Sometimes we had two or three men on the ropes to hold the steers and go in and horn them. They'd ball and bottle them. They had a regular round up there every weekend. Old Dave Gentry, he'd just tickle him to death, you know. He was a jolly old fellow anyhow.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They'd put up prices for the best riders. The rodeo. Oh yes. It wasn't only just a campaign affair. And at the dam, the lumberjacks would take and put on a burling contest. Yeah, you were telling me about that. They'd get out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Slabtown right next to Collins just about?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no, Slabtown was only about two and a half miles up here. Collins was four miles up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they real different from Bovill as towns?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were just small settlements or what?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Small settlements. When Slabtown was in there, Collins, Bovill was just one or two little houses down here. Grandma Warren, she had a couple shacks there. She had three log cabins.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And then she sold out to the Huey Bovill. He was going to make a...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he'd have coyouches for traveling, sightseeing, outfit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a resort? Is that really what he had in mind?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, that's what he had in mind. Then the potlatch come in and they wanted to buy the meadows. They put their mail, instead of at Elk River. Later on they put in Elk River. But old Bovill wanted so much for the meadows at that time. He was going to make his fortune there. He wanted so much at Old Bill various as well. He was just going up to Elk River. Of course Elk River wasn't any town, but there was a good meadow there. His cruisers told him that there was a good meadow up there and there was lots of water.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: So they put the Elk River mill in. And at that time they put in supposed to be the best mill in the world, all electrical. Of course they made their own juice and everything. And it was a wonderful mill after it got to going. They had around 2 ,400 people working there. Then they had all the logging camps out there. The logging camps was all donkeys at that time. And later on they turned to electric donkeys. They had steam at first. Then they converted them and put them into electric.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the Bovills?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they like?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They were a real accommodating old couple. They was English. And they had that accent to the English and they held their selves aloft. Of course the Englishman does that, holds himself aloft. Now Henry Mallory, he was going with one of the girls. And one night they had trouble down to one of these old shops, where the ladies were. Hook shops as they called. He went down to settle the trouble. The girl heard that Henry was down there. And boy, she put the skids on him right now. Henry was just as independent, the Featherstone was just as independent as the rest, they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a Featherstone?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, Henry was a Featherstone.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: After Frank put dairy, he took over the post office in Bovill here. He handled a post office for years here. And the Featherstone, the rest of them worked in the stores and things that was coming in new.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they stopped going together right then?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, they stopped right there. She wouldn't have nothing to do with him because she was down at the sport homes. And he went down there to quill trouble. Some of them old jacks got down there and got into trouble. He was helped out.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, look, see my supper house, it's coming.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Charlotte Bovill. I heard she was sort of the town nurse and general information source for a few years. Yeah, she was.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, Hugh used to come up and visit with me and then we'd go up on the hill here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he'd show me an old mine. Used to be over on the Rob's Creek Divide. He says he used to go up and watch him come out with an old wheelbarrow and put the dirt on the dump. He had a tunnel there. They called it the Black League.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He himself, it was his mine? It was his mine or he just watched it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no, he just watched the old fellow across the canyon, you know. He could see the old fellow come out. I went in there in later years and found the blacksmith shop and all of his tools and anvils. Everything was right in there. It's probably in there yet. If they hadn't packed it all off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did Hugh mix with the people real well?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He was quite a leader in sports and things that way. He figured on having a span of horses and taking them out on sights here in the early days.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Hikes and things that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But things kind of changed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty fast, huh?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, they changed pretty fast.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He'd have been lots better off in the country here, had been lots better off if he hadn't have been so greedy charging the Potlatch such a big exorbitant price for these metals and things here. We'd have had the mill at Elk River instead of being at Lewiston while the mill had been here yet today. Because this has always been the center point for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think maybe they wouldn't have built that mill at Lewiston if they had had the mill here instead?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I heard old Charlie Weyerhaeuser and Fred Weyerhaeuser when they was up talking to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. About the mill, putting the mill down at Lewiston, he says we'd have never put the mill at Lewiston if we had it to do over again. He says we'd have kept the mill at Elk River, which they would have. The reason the mill at Lewiston, they'd done away with the mill at Elk River. And Elk Rivers always spent a center point.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they decide to go to Lewiston and quit Elk River in the first place?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, they figured they could get all of their power and everything on the river there. And which they could, you know. They'd put in that dam and they'd run the mill right off of the dam. Of course, they'd get to have done the same thing at Elk River. That's what they'd done at Elk River. They had a good flow of water in there. Well, I wonder why at the time they thought they'd be better off at Lewiston. I'd always thought just because they had so much of that timber up in the clear water. No, no, they never had, they was bringing the timber. They never started to bring that out for later years up in there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Of course, I think when they foresee the object of the river being there, that they could flume right down to Lewiston. Another thing. But you see, all of this country in here where they've logged for all these years has come into Elk River anyhow.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And in later years, why they commenced to flume every spring, they'd flume down to the mill at Lewiston. But what I'm wondering is why there'd be a difference between building the mill at Elk River or here as far as what would have happened in the long run. Well, we was the center at that time, we was the center of their main timber. You see, a lot of this timber that they got ahold of in later years. But their main timber was around Bovill here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they had, well, they had 50 years of timber right here without moving at all. And they still got timber here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I had heard that somebody say that Hugh Bovill didn't like the idea of the land getting changed around. He thought it'd be, he was sorry to see that the pot lights come in and everything changed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was any truth to that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, I don't know about that. But he wasn't very cooperative with them when they come in here in one of the meadows and stuff. He wouldn't, he'd cooperated with them if they had to give him his price. But they wouldn't give him his price. Of course, that might have turned him, I don't know, might have turned him that he wasn't cooperative with them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But I don't think it was because he built that hotel. You see, he only built half of it. Then he built, later on, he built the other half over to live in and run the hotel. And then when the other outfit took over the hotel, they joined the old house and the hotel together and made one building stand there yet.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They joined them together and they'd give them a nice hotel for that time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, you know, you said to me that the warehouses were common people in a way, and it made me wonder, thinking about it, how people that got to be so rich could still be common people. Well, they was old stock of the people. There wasn't anything airy about them at all. They started out back there in Minnesota. They'd work and they'd put every nickel into the land. They could buy it for practically nothing. Then they commenced to build up. Well, they, now when they'd come out and visit here when they first started out, they'd come out about every year and fished around. They'd eat right along at the, they'd fix them a special table, not a special, but the same table, but it'd be up at one end. They'd eat right along with the lumberjacks, talk with them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And you'd take old Billy Watts and my father and old T .P. Jones. They was doing a good job. They'd go and buy them a suit and clothes. They'd come out, show their appreciation.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And anybody could talk to them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They, it wasn't stuck up at all.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Billy Watts was running the incline when they came in about one of the last times I remember.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the incline shut down until they seen the engine coming around the bend. This was bringing them up to camp and they started the incline up. Started it up and it went, while they stayed there, it went through perfect. The logs were just coming in, stepping in full. The morn started out down the bauble here and got around the bend and it broke down. Well, they never, never started to stop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What incline is this now? Is this the endless line they bring?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, it's the endless line.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The warehouses pronounced it the greatest invention that was ever invented for logging.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But it cost, oh at that time it cost $100 ,000 or $150 ,000 to experiment with it. Of course I think old Jones covered up a lot of expense too. But they didn't care about the expense. They thought it was a great deal. Then they commenced logging with teams and donkeys. They'd skid them into the track and load them with old marians and slides. If they got four cars logged a day at the camp, they were doing good. From the woods, the old donkeys and rigging sliders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This, the endless line, where was that at that they put that up?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That is on the beaver's boot here. They logged the whole side of the mountain with the endless line. But they'd log a little while and it'd break down. Then it'd take three, four days to fix it up again and log a little more and it'd break down. It got too expensive and they quit it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how is that different from the, was that real different from the regular high -lead logging that they did?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes. Yes, it was just, the cable started in at the donkey here and went around, clear around the big circle. Then they had another, the cable come on down to the donkey here, you see. Well, they had one donkey, one drum would come in and the other would go out. All one line. One would wind up while the other was going out and the other wind in when the other was going the other way. Then they'd hustle and they'd let these lines down and they'd take chokers and tie the logs onto this carrier. Then they'd give them a sign and they'd, the donkey would tighten up on the main line and lift them up in the air and they'd start to come on in until the next landing. Then they'd whistle them down again and they'd loosen up the line and they'd go down and put on some more logs. Well, they had these lines when the warehouses were here. They had the logs right at the line so that they could stop and whistle and pick up the logs and keep right of going to show off. Well, they come down and bought Billy Watch because he was running it. Bought him a nice suit of clothes and bragged about him and made of him. Billy laughed when they was gone.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: I come to tell you something, Granddad. I guess Kenny and Mom are going to stay over there until this weekend. They're thinking about bringing Frank home this weekend and they don't want to have to...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I think T .P.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He, of course the fire was a goin' and everything, but he wanted to get rid of Gus Wharton because Gus was the headquarters for the IWWs.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And when the fire was in one section, he'd holler to the fellas, he'd get ahold of the hose and he'd pull it down to some other place and let it burn. Hey, Wharton told him afterwards, he says, you burnt me out of purpose. Jones says you're crazy. Well, he says I got proof that you did. He says I've got proof enough to give you all kind of trouble if I wanted to give you trouble. But Wharton says I'm not a man lookin' for trouble. He says...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I'm gettin' out anyhow. And I suppose he had the place insured anyhow as far as that goes. Because Wharton was a good businessman.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: So Jones actually got ahold of... was actually one of them in on...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was he directing the firefighting?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yes. He was directing the firefighters and he'd pull the hose down from upstairs and had him turn it on other places where there wasn't any fire practically burnin', at all. Well, that's without a question, that's what he was doin'. Now Frank, my brother, when he had the trouble... when Wharton had the trouble with the... over the IWWs and everything... Now Frank was a mechanic for the blacksmith, for the potlatch and... He come down and Gus told him, he says I'll give you the place Frank, and you can pay for it as you take the money in. And he says... he says I've made around $100 ,000 clear here since I've been here. And he says you can do the same thing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And... So Frank says, well, he says I'll take it over. Now Wharton was tickled to death to get him to take it over. That was just before he burnt out. And... Jones was comin' up to Camp 8 and Frank and I was walkin' down home here. And he met Frank and he says, Frank... He says I understand you're takin' over Wharton's place. Yes, Frank says I intend to. He says it's... Wharton has made me an awful good proposition and he says I'm gonna take it over. He says, Frank, he says I'll fire every man that comes in there. Every lumberjack comes into your dump, he says I'll fire him. He says Wharton is fightin' me with fire and he says I'm gonna fight him back with fire. And he says I'm gonna run him out of town. Well, Frank says I don't see why you'd hold it against me takin' over the place. He says he's sellin' me the place so I can pay for it just as I make it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And old T .P. says he's fought me with fire ever since he's been in here and he says I'm goin' back at him. He says he's not gonna sell that place. He says I'll see to that. He says I'll fire every man that goes in there and trades with you. And Frank backed out. He wouldn't, told us he wouldn't take it. He told us about it. Well, when the fire broke out, Jones was right there pullin' the hose around. There's no question but what Jones did, pull him out so he would burn down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the fire might have been set or you think it was an accident?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I don't know. I think it was probably a cigarette was dropped but old Jones happened to be in town. Took advantage of it. Took advantage of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think Gus Verton wanted to sell his place because the heat was on him from Jones?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. That's why Gus wanted to get out. He says, as far as the IWW goes, he says it was just a meeting place. And he says I've got to cater to the working man. And he says they met in here right along and he says I was gettin' my trade from him. And he says they wanted to meet and he says I rented them the pool hall ever so often for their meetings. Well, that's what Jones wanted to do. He wanted to get rid of the IWWs. They had a bull pen, you know, over at Moscow. And they'd round up ever so often they'd round up the IWWs whether they'd done anything or anything at all. And take them over and keep them a few days and then turn them loose.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the camps, the camps up here were filthy. They had bed bugs. You couldn't go to sleep and there was pigs in under the camps, running right in the camps. And they raised their own pigs for butchering them all, theirselves, you know, for the meat. And the IWW, when the state inspector come in, Jones met them with a shea and took them up on a flat card. He had one camp fixed up, first class shape. The rest of the camps was all filthy. And he took them up and showed them the camps and they was really dignit, they thought the camps were fine. Well, they was smooth enough that they kept them there until late at night and then they had to get down. And the IWW called them and now it says you've inspected these camps. This camp is special for you fellas. But he says we want you to go to these other camps and we demand that you go. And they went up there and boy they shut the camps down right now. Old Bob Jones had 150 hogs running in under the bunkhouses. They saw cars, you know. And the mud was that deep. And in the summer evenings by the, you know, how a hog smells. Well, the smell had raised up there and then I've seen them pick up water out of the creek where there was hogs, oh, 100 yards above them. It was getting into the water. They'd pick up the water to make lemonade and stuff out of the creek. They had a platform fixed out in the creek, you know, and they'd dip the water up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, IWW's made them clean it all up. They put the clamps on them and of course that was the thing that teed OTP off so.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Because the IWW's had made them clean up their camps. That's one thing that the IWW's did do. They made them clean up their camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about these bed bugs?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I've heard that they were, that it was real hard to avoid getting them. Oh, they was terrible. I've gone in. I was railroading at the time. I'd gone in, we'd pull in one, two o 'clock at night from bringing the logs in. Put them on the sidings. I've gone in to lay down and I felt something crawling on me and I turned my flashlight on and I bet I could have counted 150 bed bugs. Calling over. Well, I got up and went out behind a cook stove in the kitchen. They kept fire all night for the cooks. Laid down between the wall and the stove where it was warm and I'd sleep out there. And yet I was firing on the railroad and tired. And you take their pillows. I've seen their pillows just dotted all over with blood where they'd smash these bed bugs. Stink. You look at the mattress. I've seen patches of bed bugs that are big around on the mattresses. Well, the potlatch, they made such a rumpus about it that the potlatch went to put in creosote. They'd paint the shacks with creosote and spray it all around. Well, I'd have done a wave with a lot of them, but it didn't do waves all up. And then the IWWs called for beds and a mattress, a sleeping mattress, cots, you know. And they made them furnish the blankets and wash them once a week. The laundry come in once a week and get their blankets, get them fresh blankets, and they badly got rid of the bed bugs. But I'm right here to tell you, the lice and bed bugs was terrible. You couldn't stay there but you'd get lousier in a pet coop. And they, mother run a boarding house in potlatch. Oh, that was in 1908. And they'd come down out of these camps and they'd stop there. Well, mother'd make them take a bath and take their clothes and put them boiling scalding water, for she didn't let them stay at the boarding house. She wouldn't tolerate it at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you keep them out that way?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, yeah, they... Well, this time you was on the railroad and you stopped and slept in the bunkhouse there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that one of the only times you had to do that, or was that a normal everyday?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, that was the railroad, you see. Wherever, wherever camps that we were stationed at, that was our headquarters. Of course, we'd come in here at night.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But if we was, like, say, over on the other side of the divide over at McClarky, we'd have to stay in the camp until the next morning. But our headquarters was at home here. But we'd be 10 days at a time, we'd never be able to get home. Well, you must have encountered that a lot, I mean, to run up against the bed bugs and stuff. Yes. And old camp 6, old camp 6, was the same way. Our headquarters was down there on the week nights while we stayed at camp 6. And then on Saturday nights, we'd come in home, open a company, and we'd go into the shop and repair the engines and things that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, what's the deal about this?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: It sounds to me like for common people, the warehouses didn't care too much about improving conditions in the camps until it was forced on them. Well, they was back east. They only come out about once a month or once every six months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So whose responsibility was it for these crud?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: T .P. Jones. He was running the camps, you see. He was running the... well, he was the superintendent of the woods.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But... They... they finally... when Nagel took over, Nagel went through the camps and he really cleaned them up. He put in lady flunkies into the camps and put in special bunkhouses. They made the bunkhouses that would hold four men. That's all they'd allow in there. And he made them keep clean.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And... The first camp was up... old camp 2 was up above Bovill here. Well, it was on cars. The camps was on cars. And the bunkhouses, instead of having them old wooden bunks and things, they had these steel cots. And he just cleaned out all that bagbug stuff and re -used disinfectant and stuff around in the bunkhouses and got rid of the bugs and put in new mattresses and stuff that way and he really cleaned the camps up. Do you think he did this because the IWW had put pressure on... Oh, well, at that time, yes, they was the instigators of the Potlatch having to clean the camps up. Because they... they showed old Jones up for not cleaning the camps up. And that's the reason old T .P. was death on them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I've heard that most of the men in the camps were Wobblies there at one time. Oh, yes, they were. They were all Wobblies. I was scaling at old camp 2.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The Wobblies had come in the bunkhouse. Well, I was keeping time. That's your scaler had to keep time at that time. Get out, Frisky.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they was old Wobblies there. They were good men. All they wanted was just a fair thing to clean up the camps so that they didn't live like hogs. And he says, I'll tell you, Sonny. He says, I won't get to see it, but he says you will see the day that these camps are just as nice to stay in as your home is. And he says, that's what we want. He says, we want things halfway decent. Well, I says, I can't blame you for that. I says, I know I have stayed around these camps and the bedbugs and the lice are terrible.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the wood rats around the barns with the horses. The rats is... well, they take old camp 8. They took clubs and went in there 3, 4 of the foreman's and they killed two barrels of wood rats. Throw them in a barrel, had them hauled off. The next morning they had two barrels full of wood rats. Well, you know what they are. They stink terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They killed them when at night?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. They'd get in there with lights and they was out into the grain, you see. With clubs and they just killed them right and left. An old TP, old Doc Connors, was a doctor. He came over to the mine and I was over there. Went over for the weekend and Dad run the mine. TP was one of the big stockholders.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: TP was telling him about killing two barrels of wood rats about when they killed them here. Old Doc was a happy old Golocchi fella.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, TP?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He was laughing, going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, TP, what did you do with that club you killed rats with? Oh TP, he says, what do you want to know what I've done with that club?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he says, that's quite a relic. He says, I want to put it on exposition.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, old Jones was telling the truth, but old Doc, you know, he was retired. He married Mrs. Laird after Laird died. He was a rich old codger and he came up there for a vacation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you think the deal with TP was? Do you think he didn't care about the welfare of the men very much?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just don't?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, but it was, they didn't have any disinfectants at that time. And they carried their beds on their back and from one camp to the other, well, if they got rid of the bed bugs in one camp, the next time a man come in there from one of the other camps, that's why he, bedding was lousy with bed bugs. And they multiply like everything. And that's the way they traveled. But if... Well, TP was awful good with these men. He'd catch a man not working or anything. He'd fire him.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he'd fire him out of camp. I don't want you in here at all. Well, he'd go into town. He'd go downtown that night.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He'd meet these jacks on the street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you fellas doing down here tonight?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, you confounded old rep of bait. You know what we're doing down here. You can't just run us out of camp. He'd grab him and show his ghost. He'd say, well, I'll tell you. He says, you go up and tell old Tom Mallard what I said to put you on. He'd hire him right in the back.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And if the man had come into camp and they didn't have any work, they could stay there two, three days and bunk up. Lots of them slept in the barns, you know, and they bunk up and they didn't charge him anything for their food. Lots of fellas would go from one camp to the other until they did get a job. Never camp they'd go into where they'd stay three, four days. Didn't have to pay anything for it. And eat like kings as far as that goes. They had steaks that big around, you know. And all kinds of cakes and cookies. I remember I've heard guys that got into the habit of doing that called camp inspectors. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you heard them call that too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, they sure changed. After Nagel got in, he cut all that stuff out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No more free meals, you mean?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No more free meals. No more clothes for the foreman. Before that, every year they'd go to the warehouse and they'd fit them all out in wool shirts, wool pants, wool socks, German rubbers, mackinole pants and jumpers, gloves. They'd give them two, three dozen pair of gloves that they could take up and keep at their room. But after Nagel come in, he cut out all that stuff. Nagel was tight.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He didn't care for nobody, only himself. And now his own brother was working in the camp, or come up there to camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, Claire, haven't you got a job that you can put me out on?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, he says I got a job for you. He says, number two, scoop here. That's a small shovel. He says, go down and go to work on the railroad. Well, his brother felt highly indignant and he swore at him and he says, you just keep your old job. He says, I can get work. He says, I don't have to work for you. And Nagel says, that's fine dandy with me. Why that number two scoop wasn't a very good job as far as the brother was concerned. Oh, no, shovel and pick. Oh.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Nagel was partial to his men. If he liked you, you was fine dandy. But if he didn't like you, you wouldn't work for the potlatch. You wouldn't have your own at all. He was awful partial that way. Now, during the Depression, the town was starving to death here. I've seen people writing the town starving to death.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I took down, we had cattle. When we'd butcher, I'd take down different families. Roasts and steaks. And I had lots of potatoes and apples and stuff that way I'd trade for. I couldn't get no money, but I'd trade for it. And I'd distribute around. But Nagel had three sons, and he kept every one of them during the Depression working. And here, a married man in town was starving to death. It was right down to hotcakes and gravy. I had a family up here, lived up on the hill right here. All in the world, they had before I found it out. For over two weeks, it's just flour, no salt or sugar to make syrup or anything. And they'd make gravy out of their flour and hotcakes three times a day. They wasn't saying a word. Another family up at... Well, there were several families up here at Slabtown that I went up there. I had beans. I was cutting wood. And getting it out to the road for two and a half a cord for Tamarack wood, 16 -inch wood. You can imagine. And I was getting out eight cords of wood a day. They'd skidded in. I had a pop saw. I saw these blocks, and then we'd build a man. It was hauling. They had 150 or 200 horses here in the camp, and they'd haul their grain in with a lowboy. And then he'd stop and pick up eight cords of wood. I'd help him load it. I had two men helping me. Were you doing this for potlatch, or were you doing this as a... Thereself. There wasn't a work...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was doing the skidding?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: My brother, John, over here, he had a team. We had the team together. And we had toms and skidded rigins.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were getting up all that wood?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, picking up that dead Tamarack and cutting it up. Damon couldn't get no cash out of it himself, but he'd take it down to Genesee. He had a farm down at Genesee. And he'd trade it to those farmers for... Like I wanted a pig. They'd butcher me a pig. Butcher it out. I wanted coffee. He'd trade it and get me 20 pounds of coffee at a time. And sugar. But very little. The only cash I got was a few dollars that Damon himself had given me to buy gas with for my saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were getting food, and that was it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I was getting food. I had a root seller out here. I had 40, 50 sacks of apples and 40, 50 sacks of potatoes. And I had a hog, and I had lard come up 100 gallons or 100 pounds of lard at the time. The farmers had drained their lard, you know, and they would stick with the death to get the wood, and I would stick with the death to get the food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this meat that you had that you were giving people, was that from your own supply?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you were just giving it away to people that needed it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. And they...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They stole on an average these people. Now, there was one family up at Slabtown there. I knew we'd miss a cow. They had a steer now and then, and Dad knew where they was going all right, but he says they've got to have something to eat. He says you can't fold them up, and he'd never say anything, as long as they didn't steal the whole bunch of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you were just giving away meat to people out of your own?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. Yes, there wasn't any money in the country. Then, June Kosher, one of the prettiest girls that ever come into this town, a beautiful girl, well educated. Her father was a... Well, he never got to be running in Boise there, but he worked under the Boise. He was a judge, and he raised a girl and a boy, and this girl was always his beautiful thing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And she got married, and they never had any work. Well, I met her one time, and I kind of surmised that it was pretty hard up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I asked her, I says, June, wouldn't you like a nice roast and a couple steaks?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, she says I'd be tickled to death to get them. Well, I took her down. A nice big roast and a couple steaks, but I had no idea that they was as hard up as they were.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And her husband married a brother of the fella that wrote Smiles. There in California.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's your sister, you mean?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Her husband, his brother, was a piano player there, and a music man there in Los Angeles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he wrote Smiles?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he wrote Smiles. He made his fortune on that song. And he sent for June, and her father and his brother to come down, send him the money to come down on. Well, they went down. June was sick when she left here. She went down, and she went right to the hospital. The doctors pronounced it malnutrition. Said that she's so far gone, starved to death. He pronounced it. He says you're starved to death. He says, I don't think there's much chance for you. And she died in just a few days. There she's starved to death here. Too proud to let it be known.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about her husband? Couldn't he get any work and food for the family?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There wasn't any work. The potlatch shut down tight, and even the stores wouldn't put out no credit to anybody. They're old customers that paid up. And if they didn't pay up, they got no more credit. Their family's living right here now. That old John Groh, he kept an awful bunch of them. He wiped over $100 ,000 off of the books. Of debts that was run during the Depression, but he kept on going. And he gives lots of them credit that never had a nickel, or never had no chance to pay.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's one thing they can give old John Groh credit for. He kept a lot of them alive here. But at that, they was lots of families that was too proud to let it be known that they was hard up that way. That's why Nogel, they was lots of people off of Sore over Nogel that way. They had no respect for him, whatever, because he kept his three sons working when there was families right here in town. He worked the very worst way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that government, that WPA, that public works?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, they never come in until after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was before?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: WPA never came in until Depression was practically over.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: This fellow over at Moscow was running it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is his name? What's it called? Office there? Or picture show? Kenworthy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Kenworthy was the head of it. And he was pretty hard -boiled too, for some of the people to get stuff. They'd go over there, that is after it started, to get stuff, and they had to have certain potentials to show and everything. I suppose the government held him responsible too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Depression or rationing in the war?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: It was the last part of the Depression. And then when Roosevelt took over and they brought the CCs out, I'll tell you, there was an awful pitiful, that was in 1933 and 1934, 1935. There was some awful pitiful sites here that them kids come out and they had pants they could pull clear around on one side of them, and shoes is that much too long for them, and old ragged shirts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are we going to get your stuffers started? They did actually go on strike, didn't they around here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yes, when the potlatch wouldn't cooperate with them and get them a fair shake like cleaning up the camps and everything, they walked out. They wouldn't go to those camps.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But the Wobblies got blamed for old fires and stuff that wasn't the more guilty or you or I were. They had a fire get started and get away from them. Well, Wobbly started it. Well, that is all propaganda. The Wobblies didn't start a fire, and they was against the fire. I worked with hundreds of them on these fires and go down the trails with dust that deep and you come down over the mountain and every time you take a step at your run just like water. And it was the dirtiest thing that you ever seen when you got off one of those fires. I was pretty lucky. I was pretty handy around the kitchen always, and they grabbed me and put me in the cookhouse. Well, I've gone like old camp too. The fire was coming across and old camp too there. We made lemonade with the barrels. We had two big barrels, 50 gallon barrels, and we had ice and nice cold drinks. But they were pigs that I knew was in the water a couple hundred yards up from where we was dipping water out of what we called the spring.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But they didn't kill anybody, but at the same time it wasn't good.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I'll tell you, these camps in the early days had everything the Wobblies throw at them coming to them because they were filthy. You'd go into old Bob Jones' camp and those pigs and the sun had been shining. It was hot anyhow. And the steam off of them had come up and you'd throw up. An outsider would come in and it'd make you almost throw up. It'd gag you that much. But you know how old pigs are. They're terrible. I never had a living with them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, the Wobblies got blamed for lots of stuff. Old Pat Malone. Some of the fellas would get drunk.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says there, Wobblies, the old summer fritters would come over and they'd round up a bunch of them that had been drinking, having a good time, and take them over and throw them in a bullpen over there at Moscow.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: All uncalled for, tall. Do you remember any of the, and I heard that the Wobblies, I've heard people say that they had some philosophy about better, I mean sure they wanted better conditions, but I heard that they believed in workers having power. No, no. Not really true. No. No. Now it's this old fella I was telling you. I talked lots with him. He says, I'll tell you, Sonny, he says, I may never see it, but he says the times are common when they'll clean these camps up. And he says, the Wobblies is the only man, the working man. He didn't call him Wobblies. He says, the working man is the only man that can make them clean up. And he says, you'll see the day that these camps are cleaned up. Well, he was right. She wants to talk to you. Yeah. Okay. I should get going then. I can get going and we'll come back. I'll come back and we'll finish up next time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to call him, don't you?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Getting pretty late anyway, so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he had supper?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, but if I had supper here, um...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: TV was telling him about killing two barrels of wood rats about when they killed him here. Old Doc was a happy old glucky fella.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, TB?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He was laughing, going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, TB, what'd you do with that club you killed rats with? Oh, TB says, what do you want to know what I've done with that club?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he says, that's quite a relic. He says, I want to put it on exposition.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, old Jones was telling the truth, but old Doc, you know, he was retired. He married Mrs. Laird after Laird died. He was a rich old codger and he came up there for a vacation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you think the deal with TB was?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Do you think that it was just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he didn't care about the welfare of the men very much?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just stubbed him?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, but it was... They didn't have any disinfectants at that time. And they carried their beds on their back and from one camp to the other, well, if they got rid of the bedbugs in one camp, the next time a man come in there from one of the other camps, the bedding was lousy with bedbugs. And they multiply like everything. And that's the way they traveled. But if... The TB was awful good with these men. He'd catch a man not working or anything. He'd fire him.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he'd fire him out of camp. I don't want you in here at all. Well, he'd go into town. He'd go downtown that night. He'd meet these jacks on the street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you fellas doing down here tonight?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, you confounded old rap bait. You know what we're doing down here. You can this and run us out of camp. And he'd grab him and show his ghost teeth and he'd say, well, I'll tell you. He says, you go up and tell old Tom Mallard. I said to put you on. He'd hire him right in the back.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And if the man had come into camp and they didn't have any work, they could stay there two, three days. And bunk up. Lots of them slept in the barns, you know. And they'd bunk up and they didn't charge him anything for their food. Lots of fellas would go from one camp to the other until they did get a job. And every camp they'd go into, they'd stay three, four days. Didn't have to pay anything for it. And eat like kings as far as that goes. They had steaks that big around, you know. And all kinds of cakes and cookies. I remember I've heard guys that got into the habit of doing that called Camp Inspectors. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you heard them call that too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, they sure changed. After Nagel got in, he cut all that stuff out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No more free meals, you mean?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No more free meals. No more clothes for the foreman. Before that, every year they'd go to the warehouse and they'd fit them all out. Wool shirts, wool pants, wool socks, German rubbers, Mackinac pants, jumpers. Gloves. They'd give them two, three dozen pair of gloves that they could take up and keep at their room. But after Nagel come in, he cut out all that stuff. Nagel was tight.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He didn't care for nobody, all himself. And now his own brother was working in the camp, or come up there to camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, Claire, haven't you got a job that you put me out on?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, he says I got a job for you. He says, number two, scoop here. That's a small shovel. He says, go down and go to work on the railroad. Well, his brother felt highly indignant and he swore at him and he says, you just keep your old job. He says, I can get work. He says, I don't have to work for you. Well, Nagel says, that's fine dandy with me. Why that number two scoop wasn't a very good job as far as a brother was concerned. Oh, no. Shovel and pick.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And Nagel was partial to his men. If he liked you, you was fine dandy. But if he didn't like you, you wouldn't work for the potlatch. He wouldn't have your own at all. He was awful partial that way. Now, during the Depression, the town was starving to death here. I've seen people right in the town starving to death.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I took down, we had cattle. When we'd butcher, I'd take down different families. Roasts and steaks. And I had lots of potatoes and apples and stuff that way. I'd trade for it. I couldn't get no money, but I'd trade for it. And I'd distribute around. But Nagel had three sons and he kept every one of them during the Depression working. And here, a married man in town was starving to death. It was right down to hotcakes and gravy. I had a family up here, lived up on the hill right here. All in the world, they had before I found it out. For over two weeks, it's just flour, no salt or sugar to make syrup or anything. And they'd make gravy out of their flour and hotcakes three times a day. They wasn't saying a word. Another family up at... Well, there were several families up here at Slabtown that I went up there. I had beans. I was cutting wood. And getting it out to the road for two and a half a cord for Tamarack wood, 16 -inch wood. You can imagine. And I was getting out eight cords of wood a day. They'd skidded in. I had a pop saw. I saw these blocks and then we'd build a man. It was hauling. They had 150 or 200 horses here. They'd haul their grain in with a lowboy. Then he'd stop and pick up eight cords of wood. I'd help him load it. I had two men helping me. Were you doing this for potlatch or were you doing this as a... We had to work it out ourselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was doing the skidding?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: My brother, John, over here. We had the team together. And we had toms and skidded riggings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were picking up all that wood?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, picking up that dead Tamarack and cutting it up. Damon couldn't get no cash out of it himself. But he'd take it down to Genesee. He had a farm down at Genesee. He'd take it down to those farmers for... Like I wanted a pig. They'd butcher me a pig. Butcher it out. I wanted coffee. He'd trade it and get me 20 pounds of coffee at a time. And sugar. But very little. The only cash I got was a few dollars that Damon himself had given me to buy gas with for my saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were getting food and that was it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I was getting food. I had a root cellar out here. I had 40, 50 sacks of apples and 40, 50 sacks of potatoes. And I had a hog and I had lard come up 100 gallons or 100 pounds of lard at a time. The farmers had drained it out, you know. And they would stickle to that to get the wood and I would stickle to that to get the food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this meat that you had that you were giving people, was that from your own supply?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you were just giving it away to people and eating it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. And they...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They stole on an average these people. Now there was one family up at Slabtown there. I knew we'd miss a cow or steer now and then. And Dad knew where they was going all right. But he says they've got to have something to eat. He says you can't fold them up and he'd never say anything. As long as they didn't steal the whole bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you were just giving away meat to people out of your own?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Out of your own. Which you probably didn't have. Oh, well, yes. Yes, there wasn't any money in the country. Then June Kosher, one of the prettiest girls that ever come into this town, was a beautiful girl. Well educated. Her father was a... Well, he never got to be running in Boise there, but he worked under the Boise. He was a judge.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he raised a girl and a boy. And this girl was always this beautiful thing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: She got married and they never had any work. Well, I met her one time and I kind of surmised that it was pretty hard up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I asked her, I says, June, wouldn't you like a nice roast and a couple steaks?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, she said I'd be tickled if I had to get them. Well, I took her down. A nice big roast and a couple steaks, but I had no idea that they was as hard up as they were. And her husband married a brother of the fella that wrote Smiles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There in California? Her husband's sister, you mean?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Her husband, his brother, was a piano player there. And a music man there in Los Angeles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he wrote Smiles?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he wrote Smiles. He made his fortune on that song. And he sent for June and her father and his brother to come down, send him the money to come down on. Well, they went down. June was sick when she left here. Went down and she went right to the hospital. The doctors pronounced it malnutrition. Said that she's so far gone, starved to death. He pronounced it. He says you're starved to death. He says, I don't think there's much chance for you. And she died in just a few days. There she'd starve to death here. Too proud that that'd be known.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about her husband? Couldn't he get any work and food for the family?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There wasn't any work. The potlatch shut down tight. Even the stores wouldn't put out no credit to anybody. Only their old customers that paid up. And if they didn't pay up, they got no more credit. Their family's living right here now. That old John Groh, he kept an awful bunch of them. He wiped over $100 ,000 off of the books. Of debts that was run during the Depression. But he kept on going and he gave lots of them credit that never had a nickel or never had no chance to pay.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's one thing they can give old John Groh credit for. He kept a lot of them alive here. But at that there was lots of families that was too proud to let it be known that they was hard up that way. That's why Noggin, there was lots of people off of Sore over Noggin that way. They had no respect for him, whatever. Because he kept his three sons working when there was families right here in town. He worked the very worst way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that government, that WPA, that public work state?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, they never come in until after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was before?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: WPA never come in until Depression was practically over.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: This fellow over at Moscow was running it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is his name?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's it called? Office there? Or picture show? Kenworthy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Kenworthy was the head of it. He was pretty hard -boiled too for some of the people to get stuff. They'd go over there, that is after it started, to get stuff. They had to have certain potentials to show and everything. I suppose the government held him responsible too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Depression or rationing in the war?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: It was the last part of the Depression. Then when Roosevelt took over and they brought the CCs out, I'll tell you, there was some awful pitiful, that was in 1933 and 1934, 1935. There was some awful pitiful sites here that them kids come out and they had pants they could pull clear around on one side of them, and shoes is that much too long for them, and old ragged shirts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are we going to get your supper started? They did actually go on strike, didn't they around here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yes, when the potlatch wouldn't cooperate with them and get them a fair shake like cleaning up the camps and everything, they walked out. They wouldn't go to those camps.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But the Wobblies got blamed for old fires and stuff that wasn't the more guilty or you or I were. They had a fire get started and get away from them. Well, Wobbly started it. Well, that is all propaganda. The Wobblies didn't start a fire. They were against fire. I worked with hundreds of them on these fires and go down the trails with dust that deep and you come down over the mountain and every time you take a step at a run just like water. And it was the dirtiest thing that you ever seen when you got off one of those fires. I was pretty lucky. I was pretty handy around the kitchen always and they grabbed me and put me in the cookhouse. Well, I had gone like old camp two. The fire was coming across and old camp two there. We made lemonade with the barrels. We had two big barrels, 50 gallon barrels. And we had ice and nice cold drinks. But they were pigs that I knew was in the water a couple hundred yards up from where we was dipping water out of what we called the spring.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But they didn't kill anybody but at the same time it wasn't good.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I'll tell you, these camps in the early days had everything the Wobblies threw at them coming to them because they were filthy. You'd go into old Bob Jones' camp and those pigs and the sun had been shining. It was hot anyhow and the steam off of them had come up and you'd throw up. An outsider would come in and it'd make you almost throw up. It'd gag you that much. Well, you know how old pigs are. They're terrible. I never had a living with them. No, the Wobblies got blamed for lots of stuff. Old Pat Malone. Some of the fellas would get drunk. Ha, ha, ha, he says there. Wobblies. Old summer fritters would come over and they'd round up a bunch of them that had been drinking, having a good time, and take them over and throw them in a bullpen over there at Moscow.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: All in call for it all. Do you remember any of the, and I heard that the Wobblies, I've heard people say that they had some philosophy about better, I mean sure they wanted better conditions, but I heard that they believed in workers having power. No, uh -huh. Not really true. No. No. Now it's this old fella I was telling you. I talked lots with him. He says, I'll tell you, Sonny, he says, I may never see it, but he says the times are common when they'll clean these camps up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he says the Wobblies is the only man, the working man. He didn't call them Wobblies. He says the working man is the only man that can make them clean up. And he says, you'll see the day that these camps are cleaned up. Well, he was right. She wants to talk to you. Mm -hmm. Yeah. Okay. I should get going then. I can get going and we'll come back. I'll come back and finish up next time. He's getting pretty late anyway, so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you had supper?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, but if I had supper here, uh...
